annotations.text: Because the finding of proctitis is always abnormal and indicates an underlying disease process, I will develop my differential diagnosis around entities that could cause proctitis in the context of this patient’s perianal findings and epidemiologic risk factors.
If we rule out all these entities from our differential diagnosis, we are left with Crohn’s disease and a number of infectious diseases.
the presence of other anorectal abnormalities in this patient raises doubts about whether he really had hemorrhoids.
On the basis of the patient’s sexual history, syphilis is more likely than inflammatory bowel disease.
Several features of this patient’s presentation are suggestive of Crohn’s disease, which is relatively common, with an incidence of approximately 10 cases per 100,000 persons.
When considering a diagnosis of infection, a timeline from the exposure to the onset of symptoms can be a useful tool in formulating a differential diagnosis.
the features and timeline in this case are consistent with syphilis.
The onset of secondary syphilis typically occurs 4 to 12 weeks after exposure; although symptoms typically resolve in 3 to 12 weeks, they may last for months.
In a review of cases in which MSM presented to a surgery clinic for treatment of proctitis, syphilis was a more common cause than Crohn’s disease.
Overall, the features and timeline in this case are consistent with syphilis.
I cannot distinguish between syphilis and Crohn’s disease. Both diseases cause proctitis, fissures, and perianal papules.

It is particularly important to consider lymphogranuloma venereum as a diagnosis in this patient, because it is an emerging cause of proctitis in the population of MSM worldwide and is responsible for several outbreaks in Europe in the past 15 years.
patient, he had multiple large fissures or ulcers, which prompted me to perform the sigmoidoscopy, as I would do in any patient with new anorectal findings
In assessing any patient with an anorectal concern, the physical examination is very informative.

Typical anal fissures are splits or ulcers in the epithelium of the lower anal canal that are usually associated with relative anal stenosis resulting from a lifetime of insufficient dietary fiber or sometimes from laxative abuse.
Between January 2013 and January 2014, the number of reported cases of syphilis increased by 15%, and in 2014, a total of 83% of men with primary and secondary syphilis were in the MSM population.

In order to establish the diagnosis of syphilis in this patient, I would perform a treponemal-specific serologic test for syphilis. If that test was positive, I would perform a rapid plasma reagin test.

Anorectal syphilis may be manifested by a chancre in the skin around the anus or by proctocolitis in the sigmoid colon and rectum, with bleeding, pain, and tenesmus; the chancre is usually a manifestation of primary infection and the proctocolitis is usually a manifestation of secondary infection.
